coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots doped with nitrogen as an ultrasensitive fluorescent probe for thrombin activity monitoring and inhibitor screening
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Xinxin
Li, Yue
Liu, Hongmei
Zhang, Qin
Li, Dandan
Zhu, Wanglisha
He, Yanping
Zhang, Guoqi
Zhao, Yan
coredata.dc:description: A simple and sensitive fluorometric assay based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was developed for the determination of thrombin (TB) activity in human serum samples and living cells. The novel N-CDs were prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method using 1,2-ethylenediamine and levodopa as precursors. Such N-CDs exhibited green fluorescence with excitation/emission peaks at 390/520 nm and a high fluorescence quantum yield of approximately 39.2%. H-D-Phenylalanyl-L-pipecolyl-Larginine-p-nitroaniline-dihydrochloride (S-2238) was hydrolyzed by TB to produce p-nitroaniline which was capable of quenching the fluorescence of N-CDs due to an inner filter effect. This assay was used to detect TB activity with a low detection limit of 11.3 fM. The proposed sensing method was then expanded to the TB inhibitor screening and exhibited excellent applicability. As a typical TB inhibitor, argatroban was determined in a concentration as low as 1.43 nM. The method has also been successfully employed for the determination of TB activity in living HeLa cells. This work showed significant potential for TB activity assay in clinical and biomedicine applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Inner filter effect
Fluorometric assay
Thrombin
Inhibitor screening
Intracellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: IFE-based fluorescent detection of TB activity
Principle of TB activity assay based on IFE
IFE-based fluorescence assay for TB activity

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Programs of Sichuan Province of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science and Technology Department of Sichuan Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: west light foundation of the Chinese academy of sciences
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: West Light Foundation of the Chinese Academy of Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by west light foundation of the Chinese academy of sciences (to YZ), and the Science and Technology Programs of Sichuan Province of China (No. 2021YFS0060 to YZ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of the prepared N-CDs. (B) Diameter size distribution curve of N-CDs. (C) XRD spectrum of N-CDs. (D) FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs.
(A) XPS survey spectrum of the N-CDs and the high-resolution XPS spectra of (B) C1s, (C) N1s, and (D) O1s of N-CDs.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs, excitation spectrum of N-CDs at λex = 390 nm, and emission spectrum of N-CDs at λem = 520 nm. Insert: photograph of N-CDs under the excitation of white light (Left) and UV lamp with 365 nm (Right). (B) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs with the excitation wavelength increasing from 360 to 450 nm.
Influence of (A) time of UV light irradiation, (B) pH, and (C) ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of PNA and S-2238, and excitation-emission spectra of N-CDs. (B) Changes of fluorescence spectra of N-CDs in the presence of S-2238 and S-2238 + TB. (C) UV–Vis absorption spectra of enzymatic reaction solution with different contents of TB from 0.00135 to 6.76 nM. (D) The fluorescence decay curve of N-CDs in the absence and presence of PNA (2 mM).
Influence of (A) concentration of N-CDs, (B) pH value of buffer solution, (C) reaction temperature, and (D) reaction time on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of N-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of TB and S-2238 (2 mM). (B) The plots of (F0–F)/F0 versus the concentration of TB. Inset: the corresponding linear relationship of (F0–F)/F0 versus TB concentration from 1.35 × 10−3 to 1.35 nM. F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs in the absence and presence of TB, respectively.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of N-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of argatroban, S-2238 (2 mM), and TB (1.35 nM). (B) The plots of inhibition ratio versus the logarithm of argatroban concentration. Inset: the corresponding linear relationship of inhibition ratio versus the logarithm of argatroban concentration (from 0.01 to 5 μM).

Confocal fluorescence images (A–E) and bright fields images (A′-E′) of HeLa cells incubated with N-CDs solution (0.1 mg/mL) before or after the addition of S-2238 (2 mM) and different concentrations of TB at 37 °C for 3 h under 488 nm excitation.

Confocal fluorescence images (A–H) and bright fields images (A′-H′) of HeLa cells incubated with N-CDs (0.1 mg/mL) before or after the addition of S-2238 (2 mM), TB (1.35 nM), and different concentrations of argatroban at 37 °C for 3 h under 488 nm excitation.
Schematic illustration of the detection strategy for TB activity based on N-CDs.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots doped with nitrogen as an ultrasensitive fluorescent probe for thrombin activity monitoring and inhibitor screening

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Xihua University, Chengdu, 610039, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, Xihua University, Chengdu, 610039, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: People's Hospital of Xinjin District, Chengdu Clinical Laboratory, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: People's Hospital of Xinjin District, Chengdu Clinical Laboratory

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and sensitive fluorometric assay based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was developed for the determination of thrombin (TB) activity in human serum samples and living cells. The novel N-CDs were prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method using 1,2-ethylenediamine and levodopa as precursors. Such N-CDs exhibited green fluorescence with excitation/emission peaks at 390/520 nm and a high fluorescence quantum yield of approximately 39.2%. H-D-Phenylalanyl-L-pipecolyl-Larginine-p-nitroaniline-dihydrochloride (S-2238) was hydrolyzed by TB to produce p-nitroaniline which was capable of quenching the fluorescence of N-CDs due to an inner filter effect. This assay was used to detect TB activity with a low detection limit of 11.3 fM. The proposed sensing method was then expanded to the TB inhibitor screening and exhibited excellent applicability. As a typical TB inhibitor, argatroban was determined in a concentration as low as 1.43 nM. The method has also been successfully employed for the determination of TB activity in living HeLa cells. This work showed significant potential for TB activity assay in clinical and biomedicine applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Thrombin (TB), a specific serine protease, plays an essential role in blood coagulation by promoting fibrinogen conversion to fibrin [1,2]. It is also associated with many thromboembolic diseases including arterial and vein thrombosis, myocardial infarction, atherosclerosis, and acute coronary syndrome [3–5]. Inhibition of TB activity is one of the most efficacious treatment methods for thromboembolic diseases [6,7]. However, TB inhibitors such as warfarin, heparin, and argatroban [8], which are widely applied in clinical treatments at present, have some disadvantages, including expensive production cost, drug resistance, gastro-intestinal toxicity, thrombocytopenia development [9–11]. For this reason, looking for potential TB inhibitors with less adverse reactions from natural resources has been considered an effective and feasible alternative strategy for treating thromboembolic diseases [12]. Therefore, developing an effective, sensitive method for the determination of TB and its inhibitors has been always becoming an urgent and important task in disease diagnosis and drug evaluation.

Various analytical methods have been developed for the determination of TB such as colorimetry [13,14], liquid chromatography/tandem mass spectrometry [15], surface-enhanced Raman scattering electrochemistry [16], electrochemistry [17–19], and fluorescence methods [20,21]. Among them, the fluorescence method has attracted more and more attention because it offers several advantages such as high sensitivity, low cost, simplicity, and low interference. Fluorescent assays based on TB DNA aptamers are generally used for the detection of TB, which possess high selectivity [22]. However, aptamers are high-cost and difficult to be reused. Therefore, it will be a beneficial attempt to develop a fluorescence method for TB assay without the link of an aptamer.

As a type of carbon-based nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable interest due to their easy preparation, good light stability, low photobleaching, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility compared with organic dyes, noble metal nanoclusters, semiconductor quantum dots [23,24]. Therefore, they have been widely used to establish fluorescent assays for determination of biomolecules such as β-glucuronidase, butyrylcholinesterase, α-glucosidase and β-Galactosidase [25–28]. So far, there are rare reports on the determination of TB activity based on CDs except for Hu et al. [24]. However, the reported CDs were prepared with biomass materials, which usually have low quantum yields (QYs, <20%) and poor fluorescence performance [29]. Thus, exploring an effective way to improve the QYs of CDs is still desirable and significant for the sensitive detection of TB. Indeed, small organic molecules are ideal candidates as carbon precursors for the preparation of CDs with high performance and QYs, due to their rich functional groups and light-emitting units [30].

Recently, the inner filter effect (IFE) has been employed as an effective fluorescence sensing strategy. Usually, the IFE refers to the phenomenon that the absorption spectra of the absorber overlap with the excitation or emission spectra of fluorophore in the reaction system, which will cause the fluorescence quenching of the fluorophore [31]. Compared with other fluorescence sensing mechanisms including Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photo-induced electron transfer (PET), electronic energy transfer (EET), and intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), IFE exhibits great advantages for the development of sensitive and simple fluorescent sensors because it does not require complicated surface functionalization of fluorescence material or the covalent coupling between the receptor and fluorophore [26].

In this work, a sensitive and facile fluorometric assay with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) based on IFE was developed for TB activity monitoring and its inhibitors screening. The novel N-CDs with bright green fluorescence and a high fluorescence QY of 39.2% were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method using 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) and levodopa (l-Dopa) as precursors. The fluorescent assay exhibited a sensitive response to TB activity by using H-D-Phenylalanyl-L-pipecolyl-Larginine-p-nitroaniline-dihydrochloride (S-2238) as the specific substrate. p-Nitroaniline (PNA), which is the reaction product of S-2238 through the catalysis of TB, can effectively quench the fluorescence of N-CDs through IFE (Scheme 1 ), due to the good overlap between the absorption spectrum of PNA and the excitation spectrum of N-CDs. The proposed method was used to detect TB activity in human serum samples and exhibited satisfactory accuracy. Furthermore, TB inhibitors screening was further evaluated through the established fluorescent assay using argatroban as a model compound. The imaging effects toward TB detection in HeLa cells were also investigated. Compared to the previous fluorescence methods, this method based on IFE for TB assay has advantages of operational simplicity, due to the elimination of complicated surface functionalization of fluorescence material and the absence of tedious DNA labeling or modification techniques. Therefore, this work provides a new fluorescent assay for TB activity monitoring and its inhibitors screening and exhibits wide prospects in clinical diagnosis and drug discovery.

Codonopsis pilosula, Glycyrrhiza uralensis, Angelica sinensis, Cynanchum podophyllum, Ligusticum chuanxiong, Polygala tenuifolia, Carthamus tinctorius, and Salvia miltiorrhiza were obtained from Chengdu branch of Beijing Tong Ren Tang (Chengdu, China). l-Dopa was bought from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). EDA was purchased from Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). TB was supplied by Dalian Meilun Biotech Co., Ltd. (Dalian, China). S-2238 was purchased from Adhoc International Technologies Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Normal human serum was bought from Beijing Solar Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Human serum albumin (HSA), α-glucosidase (α-GLU), α-amylase (AMS), alkali protease (AKP), lysozyme (LYZ), trypsin (TRY), and hemoglobin (HB) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Argatroban, atorvastatin, aspirin (ASP), and all other chemicals were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Human cervical carcinoma HeLa cells were provided by Hunan Fenghui Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Changsha, China). Serum samples of pregnant women were kindly donated by People's Hospital of Xinjin District Chengdu.

The N-CDs were fabricated by a one-pot hydrothermal method. In a typical synthesis procedure, l-Dopa (0.0407 g) was dissolved in 20.0 mL of ultrapure water to form a transparent solution under ultrasonication. Then, EDA (600 μL) was added into the solution under vigorous stirring for a few minutes, the obtained solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon stainless steel autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 6 h in an oven (Fig. S1). After the reaction, a light-yellow solution was collected. Subsequently, the solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove the large insoluble particles, and then the final solution was treated with the dialysis bag (1000 Da, MWCO) for approximately 24 h to remove the precursor molecules. Finally, the collected N-CDs solution was freeze-dried for further use.

Confocal microscopy imaging of cell samples was acquired on an Olympus FV3000 laser scanning confocal microscope (Olympus, Japan). UV–Vis absorption was determined with a Shimadzu UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a HORIBA FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime was measured by an FLS1000 steady state and transient state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., UK). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired on a Talos F200X field emission transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted on an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were measured on Brucker D8 Advance Powder X-ray diffractometer (Brucker, Germany). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Thermo Fisher Nicolet iS10 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: IFE-based fluorescent detection of TB activity
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed IFE-based fluorescence assay for TB was performed according to the following procedure. A total of 30 μL of different concentrations of TB solutions (1.35 × 10−3 - 6.76 nM) were added into the reaction system which consisted of 20 μL of S-2238 (2 mM), respectively. The reaction solution was vortex-mixed and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. Then, the mixture was added into 2.0 mL of N-CDs (0.1 mg/mL) buffer solution (2 mM PBS, pH 7.0) with gentle shaking for 2 min. The fluorescence spectra of the final solution were recorded at the wavelength range of 415–650 nm under the excitation wavelength of 390 nm, and the fluorescence intensity of the maximum emission peak was used for quantitative analysis. The slits of excitation and emission were both set at 7.0 nm.

Argatroban, which is a synthetic direct TB inhibitor and thrombosis treatment drug related to heparin use, was chosen as a model compound to evaluate the screening feasibility of the proposed method. The experiment of screening feasibility was performed as follows. The assay solutions containing 30 μL of 1.35 nM TB in PBS buffer (pH 7.0), 20 μL of 2 mM S-2238 solutions, and different concentrations of argatroban ranging from 0 to 10.0 μM were prepared. Subsequently, 2.0 mL of N-CDs (0.1 mg/mL) buffer solution was added after 30 min of incubation time at 37 °C. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 390 nm.

Meanwhile, the inhibiting abilities of Codonopsis pilosula, Glycyrrhiza uralensis, Angelica sinensis, Cynanchum otophyllum, Ligusticum chuanxiong, Polygala tenuifolia, Carthamus tinctorius, and Salvia miltiorrhiza were also evaluated by the proposed method. The detailed extraction process was performed as follows. Briefly, 1.0 g of powdered medicinal plants were refluxed with 40.0 mL of 80% ethanol at 80 °C for 30 min, respectively. The extraction solution was filtered and concentrated to dryness in vacuum. Then, the crude extracts were respectively redissolved in PBS (pH 7.0) for inhibiting ability evaluation.

The cytotoxicity of N-CDs on HeLa cells was evaluated by a standard CCK-8 assay. Briefly, 100 μL of cell suspension was added in 96-well plates in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM, 10% fetal bovine serum, and 1% penicillin-streptomycin) and pre-cultured at 37 °C, 5% CO2 in an incubator for 24 h. Then, 10 μL of N-CDs solution at varying concentrations (0, 30, 60, 150, 300, and 500 μg/mL) were added into the HeLa cells for further incubation at 37 °C for 24 h and 48 h, respectively. Then, 10 μL of CCK-8 solution was added into each well for further incubation of 4 h. The optical density (OD) of the wells was recorded at 450 nm. The cytotoxicity was calculated by the following equation: cell viability (%) = (ODtreated – ODblank)/(ODcontrol – ODblank) × 100, where ODblank was the absorbance of the medium solution; ODtreated and ODcontrol were the absorbances of the HeLa cells after and before the treatment with N-CDs, respectively. The OD values were the average of three replicate experiments.

For fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells treated with TB, the HeLa cells were incubated with 20 μL of S-2238 solution (2 mM) after treating with different concentrations of TB for 30 min. For fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells treated with argatroban, the HeLa cells were incubated with 30 μL of TB (1.35 nM) after treating with different concentrations of argatroban for 10 min and then treated with 20 μL of S-2238 solution (2 mM) for 30 min. Then, 2 mL of N-CDs (0.1 mg/mL) was added and incubated with HeLa cells in DMEM medium for a further 3 h. After the incubation, the HeLa cells were washed with PBS (0.01 M, pH 7.0) buffer solution for three times before confocal imaging.

The TEM image of the prepared N-CDs was shown in Fig. 1 A. It could be obviously found that the prepared N-CDs were spherical and possessed good solution-dispersion without apparent aggregation. Based on the statistical analysis of more than one hundred particles, the N-CDs exhibited narrow particle size distribution ranging from approximately 1.0 to 5.0 nm with a mean particle size of about 2.87 nm (Fig. 1B). As shown in Fig. 1C, the N-CDs displayed a wide single peak centered at around 23.9°, which was ascribed to the graphite lattice spacing (002 facets) of highly disordered carbon atoms [32].

The FT-IR spectrum was shown in Fig. 1D. The broad absorption band at 3321 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H bonds on the surface of the N-CDs. The peak at 1258 cm−1 was characteristic of the bending vibration of phenolic hydroxyl group (C–OH). In addition, the absorption peaks at 1575 and 1485 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CO and C–N bonds, respectively. The weak absorption peak at 1322 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of C–O bond. The absorption peaks at 2928 and 1396 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching and bending vibrations of C–H bond, respectively. The FT-IR results suggested that the prepared N-CDs possessed highly hydrophilic and good dispersibility in water owing to the surface polar functional groups.

The elemental composition and surface groups of N-CDs were further investigated by XPS. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the full-range XPS spectrum of N-CDs showed three typical characteristic binding energy peaks at 287.08, 400.08, and 532.08 eV, which were attributed to the signals C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum showed three binding energy peaks at 284.28, 285.58, and 286.00 eV, which were assigned to C–C/CC, C–O, and C–N/C–O functional groups, respectively (Fig. 2B). The high-resolution N 1s spectrum showed three peaks centered at 401.58, 401.08 and 399.08 eV, which were attributed to N–H, C–N and C–N–C bonds, respectively (Fig. 2C). As shown in the high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2D), three peaks at 532.78, 532.08 and 530.88 eV could be ascribed to OC–O, C–O and CO bonds, respectively. The results of XPS spectra were consistent with FT-IR results.

To explore the optical properties of the synthesized N-CDs, their UV–Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 A, N-CDs displayed two typical UV–Vis absorption peaks at 280 nm and 380 nm (labeled by the blue dotted circle), respectively. The absorption peak at 280 nm was attributed to the π→π * electronic transition of sp2 hybrid carbon, while the absorption peak at 380 nm was ascribed to the n→π * electronic transition of the amine/carbonyl groups on the surface of N-CDs [33]. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of N-CDs were 390 and 520 nm, respectively. Furthermore, the light-yellow N-CDs aqueous solution emitted bright green fluorescence under irradiation of the ultraviolet lamp with the wavelength of 365 nm (insert in Fig. 3A). However, unlike most of the carbon-based nanomaterials, the major emission wavelength of N-CDs was nearly excitation wavelength-independent with the excitation wavelength between 360 nm and 450 nm (Fig. 3B), which suggested that the particle size distribution of N-CDs was relatively uniform [26].

As shown in Fig. S2, the average fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs (excited at 390 nm) was calculated to be around 4.04 ns. The absolute fluorescence QY of N-CDs was calculated to be 39.2% by using the integrating sphere (excited at 390 nm), which was higher than the reported N-CDs [33]. Therefore, the prepared N-CDs can be used for highly sensitive fluorescence analysis and cellular imaging.

To explore the fluorescence stability of N-CDs, the effects of UV light irradiation, pH, and ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity were tested. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was almost kept unchanged under continuous 365 nm UV light irradiation for 180 min, indicating good photobleaching resistance. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity changed slightly when the pH values were in the wide range of 3–11 (Fig. 4B). Fig. 4C showed the effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs in different concentrations of NaCl. It was obvious that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was nearly constant even in the ultra-high concentration of 3.0 M. All these findings showed that the synthesized N-CDs possessed excellent and stable fluorescence even under some harsh conditions, facilitating wide applications in the fields of biosensor, analytical detection, and cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of TB activity assay based on IFE
ce:section.ce:para: In this sensing assay, S-2238 was employed as the chromogenic substrate of TB. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the maximum UV–Vis absorbance of S-2238 was located at 310 nm, which did not influence the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. However, PNA, the hydrolysis product of S-2238 by TB, showed a strong absorption peak at 380 nm, which exhibited a great overlap with the excitation spectrum of N-CDs. Therefore, PNA could quench the fluorescence of N-CDs through IFE due to the absorption of the excitation light [34].

The feasibility of this method for TB assay was displayed in Fig. 5B. There was no obvious change in fluorescence intensity after the addition of a certain amount of S-2238. However, the fluorescence was quenched greatly after the addition of TB into the mixture containing N-CDs and S-2238, indicating the feasibility of fluorometric assay based on the IFE mechanism. To further verify the feasibility of the method, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of S-2238 pretreated with TB were shown in Fig. 5C. It was obvious that the absorbance of S-2238 at 310 nm decreased while the absorbance of PNA at 380 nm increased gradually with the increment of concentration of TB from 1.35 × 10−3 to 6.76 nM.

To verify the IFE mechanism, time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of N-CDs were investigated in the absence and presence of PNA. As shown in Fig. 5D, the average fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs was kept almost constant after the addition of PNA, indicating that PNA exhibited no influence on the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs [35]. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism was proved to be IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: IFE-based fluorescence assay for TB activity
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the IFE mechanism, the prepared N-CDs can be applied as a fluorescent sensor for the direct determination of TB activity. To achieve the best sensing response, experimental parameters including the concentration of CDs, pH value of buffer solution, reaction temperature, and reaction time were investigated. The effect of the concentration of N-CDs was investigated in the range of 0.01–0.2 mg/mL. It was observed that the maximum fluorescence quenching value was achieved when the concentration of N-CDs was 0.1 mg/mL. Therefore, 0.1 mg/mL of N-CDs was selected for the following experiments (Fig. 6 A). The effect of pH was investigated by varying the pH value of PBS buffer solution in the range of 5.5–8.5 with a fixed TB concentration of 1.35 nM. The results revealed that the maximum fluorescence quenching value occurred at pH = 7.0, which was selected for the subsequent experiments (Fig. 6B). The effect of reaction temperature from 20 to 50 °C was investigated. Experimental results showed that 37 °C was the optimal reaction temperature, which was close to human body temperature (Fig. 6C). The effect of reaction time from 10 to 45 min was investigated. The results showed that the reaction time of 30 min was enough to achieve the maximum fluorescence quenching value. Therefore, 30 min was chosen as the reaction time (Fig. 6D).

Under the optimal experimental conditions, the fluorescence change of the sensing system containing N-CDs and S-2238 was recorded as the addition of different concentrations of TB. As shown in Fig. 7 A, when the TB level increased from 0 to 6.76 nM, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs decreased gradually, indicating the gradual hydrolysis of S-2238 into PNA by TB. A good linear response between the variation of fluorescence intensity of N-CDs and TB level from 1.35 × 10−3 to 1.35 nM was displayed in Fig. 7B, where F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs in the absence and presence of TB, respectively. The linear fitting for the calibration curve can be expressed as y = 0.3771x + 0.1099 with a good coefficient of determination of 0.9936. The limit of detection (LOD) for TB was estimated to be 11.3 fM at a standard deviation-to-slope (σ/S) ratio of 3, where σ was the standard deviation of 11 measurements of the blank signal of N-CDs and the S was the slope of the calibration curve. To the best of our knowledge, the performance of our method was more sensitive than those previous studies for TB activity (Table S1).

To evaluate the selectivity of this method, the interference effects of some metal ions (Zn2+, Pb2+, Na+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cu2+, Mg2+), some common biomolecules (HSA, α-GLU, AMS, AKP, LYZ, TRY, HB) and some pharmaceutical molecules (ASP, atorvastatin) on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. S3, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs appeared almost no significant variations in the presence of different metal ions (the concentration of each metal ion was 0.01 M), biomolecules (the concentration of HSA and HB was 10 mg/mL, and other biomolecules was 10 U/mL), and pharmaceutical molecules (the concentration of each pharmaceutical molecules was 20 mg/mL). These results of interference experiments demonstrated the high selectivity of N-CDs for TB detection.

Moreover, this method was applied for the determination of TB in normal human serum samples and serum samples of pregnant women. As shown in Table S2, the mean recoveries of TB in normal human serum samples were in the range of 95.8%–104.2%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of the intra-day (n = 5) and inter-day (n = 15) precision were 2.2–3.7% and 2.1–3.8%, respectively, while the recoveries of the pregnant women samples ranged from 98.4% to 102.9% and the RSDs of the intra-day (n = 5) and inter-day (n = 15) precision were 1.9–4.4% and 1.5–3.8%, respectively. In addition, the standard colorimetric method was applied for the determination of TB activity in serum samples of pregnant women compared with the proposed fluorescent method by t-test. As shown in Table S3, when the confidence level is 95% with four degrees of freedom, the t-values are all below the tabulated value (2.776), indicating that the proposed fluorescence method is statistically comparable with colorimetric methods in terms of assay accuracy. These validation data indicated the proposed fluorescent sensor based N-CDs was appropriate for monitoring TB in complex biological samples with good repeatability and reproducibility.

This fluorescent sensing assay can further be used to evaluate the potential application in TB inhibitor screening. Argatroban was selected as an example for inhibition assays. As shown in Fig. 8 A, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs increased gradually with the increment of the concentration of argatroban. The inhibition ratio (IR) could be expressed by the relevant equation of IR (%) = [(F2–F1)/(F0–F1)] × 100, where F0 stands for the initial fluorescence intensity of N-CDs in the absence of TB, and F1 and F2 stand for the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs in the presence of TB without or with argatroban, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8B, the IR increased gradually with the increment of concentrations of argatroban. The linear calibration curve was obtained between IR and the logarithm of argatroban concentration (from 0.01 to 5 μM) with R2 = 0.9942. The LOD was estimated to be 1.43 nM for argatroban at a standard deviation-to-slope (σ/S) ratio of 3, which indicated that this sensing assay was ultra-sensitivity and could be applied for TB inhibitor screening in drug discovery.

The half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50 value) of argatroban for TB was calculated to be 1.32 μM by the regression equation, suggesting the high inhibiting ability of argatroban for TB. Some natural flavonoids and polyphenols have been identified as TB inhibitors, which may be explored as candidate drugs for the treatment of cerebral hemorrhage due to their multi-target treatment, good efficacy, and little side effects [36,37]. As shown in Fig. S4, the IC50 values of the ethanol extracts of Codonopsis pilosula, Glycyrrhiza uralensis, Angelica sinensis, Cynanchum otophyllum, Ligusticum chuanxiong, Polygala tenuifolia, Carthamus tinctorius and Salvia miltiorrhiza were 18.54, 16.45, 17.44, 18.34, 9.35, 10.84, 16.33 and 14.35 mg/mL, respectively. Therefore, the proposed method could be applied for the screening of trace TB inhibitors from natural resources.

To assess the potential biomedical applications of N-CDs for intercellular detection of TB, the cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of N-CDs were assessed through incubating N-CDs with HeLa cells by CCK-8 assays. The results in Fig. S5 showed that the total cell viability of HeLa cells remained more than 90% even in the concentration of N-CDs up to 500 μg/mL for 48 h, indicating that N-CDs possessed negligible cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility to HeLa cells.

The living cellular images of these N-CDs were captured by laser scanning confocal microscopy. After incubating HeLa cells with 0.1 mg/mL of N-CDs solution at 37 °C for 3 h, N-CDs exhibited bright green fluorescence inside the HeLa cells (Fig. 9 A and 9A′). Moreover, after the addition of S-2238 to the HeLa cells, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was not significantly affected (Fig. 9B and B’). In addition, after further introduction of TB, the green fluorescence of N-CDs weakened gradually with the increment of the TB concentration (Fig. 9C–E), suggesting that S-2238 was rapidly hydrolyzed by TB to PNA which led to the following fluorescence quenching of N-CDs through IFE. TB inhibition experiments were carried out by pretreating HeLa cells with argatroban. As shown in Fig. 10 , with the increment of the concentration of argatroban, the green fluorescence of N-CDs in HeLa cells was restored gradually, demonstrating that the presence of argatroban could effectively inhibit the hydrolysis ability toward S-2238 of TB. These results indicated that N-CDs could be applied for cellular imaging and intracellular monitoring of TB.

In this study, a novel fluorometric assay method for the ultrasensitive determination of TB activity and its inhibitors was developed based on the IFE of N-CDs. S-2238 was chosen as the substrate of TB and can be hydrolyzed to PNA. The relationship between fluorescence intensity and TB level was used to establish a TB assay. N-CDs displayed negligible cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility on HeLa cells. The feasibility performance of inhibitor screening was also evaluated by taking argatroban as the model compound. The results showed low s of 11.3 fM and 1.32 μM for TB and argatroban, respectively. Therefore, the proposed method was demonstrated to be a highly sensitive and efficient sensing strategy for TB activity assay and provided a potential tool for trace TB inhibitors screening from medicinal plants.

Xinxin Jiang: Writing-original draft, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yue Li: Investigation, Methodology. Hongmei Liu: Investigation, Project administration. Qin Zhang: Investigation, Validation. Dandan Li: Investigation, Methodology. Wanglisha Zhu: Investigation, Validation. Yanping He: Resources. Guoqi Zhang: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Yan Zhao: Conceptualization, Writing-original draft, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by west light foundation of the Chinese academy of sciences (to YZ), and the Science and Technology Programs of Sichuan Province of China (No. 2021YFS0060 to YZ).
